blocks.content: The hotel was twelve minutes from the house. They'd laughed at that — the most romantic night of their lives and they were staying at the only hotel in Millhaven, a place Kit had driven past ten thousand times without once thinking about it.
None of that mattered when the door closed behind them.

They stood in the quiet of the room — the noise of the day still ringing somewhere behind them, the DJ and the dancing and the aunts and the goodbyes — and it went still. Just them. The first time in their entire lives they'd been alone in a room without a clock running somewhere.
She still had her hair up. A few pieces had come loose through the night, the way they always did, the way he'd watched happen at every summer thing since they were children. He crossed the room.
He reached up and pulled the last pin free. Her hair fell.
He tilted his head and pressed his lips to the side of her neck — slow, deliberate, the way you do something you have been imagining for longer than you want to admit.
She exhaled. Her hands found the buttons at her back.
Morning came through the gap in the curtains in a long stripe of gold. The city — such as Millhaven was a city — was already awake, already moving.

He lay on his side and looked at her the way he'd always wanted to — without the glass of a screen between them, without a phone call ending, without a goodbye. Just her. Her hair across the pillow, her hand open beside her face, the particular stillness of a person sleeping without worry.
He thought: this is what I was waiting for. Not the ceremony. Not the vows. This.
She turned toward him. He didn't look away.
